It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is also called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokushogu|1=UNKNOWN}},” and is commonly known as “Yufu Shrine” or “Yufusan Shrine.” === Enshrined Deities === The shrine enshrines the following six deities. The alternate name “Rokushogu” derives from worshipping these six deities: * {{ill|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|Q478504|WD=Q478504|lt=Kuni no Tokotachi no Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}} * {{ill|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|Q5966692|WD=Q5966692|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|1=Hoori}} * {{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Hikonagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|lt=Kamuyamato Iwarehiko no Mikoto|1=Emperor Jimmu}} (First Emperor Jimmu) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Suizei|Q313321|WD=Q313321|lt=Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto|1=Emperor Suizei}} (Second Emperor Suizei) --- === About Unaguhime === [[File:Yufuin Basin 11.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} (from the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}})
The basin was once a lake, said to have been developed by Unaguhime.]] Although the currently enshrined deities are the six listed above, the shrine name recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} is “Unaguhime Shrine,” and the conferment of divine ranks in the Six National Histories was made to “Unaguhime no Kami (Unagihime no Kami).” Therefore, it is believed that the original deity was '''Unaguhime''' (also read Unagihime or Unakihime). Regarding the name “Unaguhime,” one theory holds that “unagu” refers to a necklace such as magatama beads, suggesting that a female chieftain shaman wearing such ritual ornaments later became deified. Another theory derives the name from “unagi” (eel). There is an old legend concerning Unaguhime that the {{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} was once a lake (the “Kerisaki legend”). According to this story, Unaguhime, the deity of Mount Yufu, saw the lake spread before her and ordered a strong man to kick through its shore. When he did so, the water drained away and the lake became a basin, through which the present-day {{ill|lt=Oita River|Q11432686|WD=Q11432686|1=Ōita-gawa}} now flows. The strong man was named “Michinomi no Mikoto” and is still enshrined at the subsidiary Kerisaki Gongen Shrine. It is also said that the remnant of the dried lake became {{ill|lt=Lake Kinrin|Q11648024|WD=Q11648024|1=Lake Kinrin}}. However, archaeological and geological evidence has not confirmed the lake legend. Pottery has been excavated from the basin floor, wooden fragments dating back 23,000 years were discovered 20–30 meters underground, and no diatomaceous earth—expected if it had been a lake—has been found. As for the interpretation of the tradition, some identify Unaguhime with “Hayatsuhime,” mentioned in the {{ill|lt=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|Q11633698|WD=Q11633698|1=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki}}, while others point to the {{ill|lt=Himehiko system|Q11330202|WD=Q11330202|1=Hime-Hiko System}}. A similar tradition is also told of Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto in the Aso region. According to the ancient document {{ill|lt=Uetsufumi|Q11360118|WD=Q11360118|1=Uetsufumi}}, discovered in Oita Prefecture (Kamakura period, authored by {{ill|lt=Otomo Yoshinao|Q11433255|WD=Q11433255|1=Ōtomo Yoshinao}}, the first lord of Bungo Province, and generally considered a forgery), Unagihime was a deity governing cotton cultivation. The area was called Yufu (meaning cotton), suggesting that cotton was once cultivated there. However, cotton was introduced to Japan in the 8th century, and before that, “yū” (wooden cotton) usually referred to cloth made from tree fibers such as paper mulberry or hemp. --- === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in the 12th year of the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}. The “Shrine Register” records that Emperor Keikō conducted rituals here during his western expedition, and that in the third year of his reign, Hayatsuhime founded the shrine by imperial command. The shrine stands at the southwestern foothills of Mount Yufu. Historical sources indicate that it was originally established with Mount Yufu as its sacred object of worship (shintaisan). Another theory suggests that the shrine began with worship of eels as spirits based on the lake legend and later merged with the worship of Mount Yufu’s deity. === Overview === The shrine first appears in official history in 849 (Kashō 2), when it was granted the Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, and in 883 (Gangyō 7), it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. These ranks were conferred upon “Unaguhime no Kami.” In the mid-Heian period, the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} lists “Unaguhime Shrine” in Hayami District, Bungo Province, classifying it as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}. Until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was syncretized with {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} under the system of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinto-Buddhist syncretism|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, but following the {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, it assumed its present form. In 1873 (Meiji 6), it was designated a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in 1923 (Taishō 12), it was promoted to prefectural shrine status. === Divine Ranks === * 849 (Kashō 2), 6th month: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}) – listed as “Unaguhime no Kami.” * September 2, 883 (Gangyō 7): Promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}) – listed as “Unagihime no Kami.” --- === Shrine Precincts === The precincts exceed 10,000 tsubo and were once surrounded by ancient {{ill|lt=cedar trees|Q147388|WD=Q147388|1=Cryptomeria}}, but many were destroyed by a {{ill|lt=typhoon|Q140588|WD=Q140588|1=Typhoon}} in 1991 (Heisei 3). The main hall is a vermilion {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style structure. A large torii gate also stands in central Yufuin. File:Unaguhime-jinja honden.JPG|Main hall File:Unaguhime-jinja haiden-2.JPG|Worship hall File:Unaguhime-jinja shinmon.JPG|Approach and shrine gate File:Unaguhime-jinja torii.JPG|Torii gate --- === Subsidiary Shrines === '''Within the precincts''' * Tadasu Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Otoshi Shrine '''Outside the precincts''' * Ogo Shrine * Location: Kawaminami, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * The grounds contain many giant cedars, including the nationally designated natural monument “Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine,” over 1,000 years old and 38 meters tall (designated August 9, 1934). * Kerisaki Gongen Shrine * Location: Nakagawa, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * Enshrines the deity associated with the Kerisaki legend. --- === Cultural Properties === === National Natural Monument === * Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine – Located within the grounds of Ogo Shrine. Over 1,000 years old, 38 meters tall. Designated August 9, 1934. --- === Access Information === '''Address''' * 2220 Kawakami, Yufuin-chō, {{ill|lt=Yufu City|Q990455|WD=Q990455|1=Yufu}}, {{ill|lt=Oita Prefecture|Q133924|WD=Q133924|1=Ōita Prefecture}} '''Access''' * By train: {{ill|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|Q498366|WD=Q498366|lt=JR Kyushu|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Kyūdai Main Line|Q870569|WD=Q870569|1=Kyūdai Main Line}} – {{ill|lt=Yufuin Station|Q8060317|WD=Q8060317|1=Yufuin Station}} (approx. 15 minutes on foot) --- === References === '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="note"}} '''Sources''' {{reflist}} === Related Articles === {{Commonscat|Unaguhime-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}} * {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} – Former {{ill|lt=bettō temple|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} of this shrine. {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bungo Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大分県の神社]] [[Category:豊後国の式内社]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:由布市の建築物]] [[Category:由布市の歴史]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is also called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokushogu|1=UNKNOWN}},” and is commonly known as “Yufu Shrine” or “Yufusan Shrine.” === Enshrined Deities === The shrine enshrines the following six deities. The alternate name “Rokushogu” derives from worshipping these six deities: * {{ill|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|Q478504|WD=Q478504|lt=Kuni no Tokotachi no Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}} * {{ill|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|Q5966692|WD=Q5966692|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|1=Hoori}} * {{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Hikonagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|lt=Kamuyamato Iwarehiko no Mikoto|1=Emperor Jimmu}} (First Emperor Jimmu) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Suizei|Q313321|WD=Q313321|lt=Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto|1=Emperor Suizei}} (Second Emperor Suizei) --- === About Unaguhime === [[File:Yufuin Basin 11.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} (from the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}})
The basin was once a lake, said to have been developed by Unaguhime.]] Although the currently enshrined deities are the six listed above, the shrine name recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} is “Unaguhime Shrine,” and the conferment of divine ranks in the Six National Histories was made to “Unaguhime no Kami (Unagihime no Kami).” Therefore, it is believed that the original deity was '''Unaguhime''' (also read Unagihime or Unakihime). Regarding the name “Unaguhime,” one theory holds that “unagu” refers to a necklace such as magatama beads, suggesting that a female chieftain shaman wearing such ritual ornaments later became deified. Another theory derives the name from “unagi” (eel). There is an old legend concerning Unaguhime that the {{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} was once a lake (the “Kerisaki legend”). According to this story, Unaguhime, the deity of Mount Yufu, saw the lake spread before her and ordered a strong man to kick through its shore. When he did so, the water drained away and the lake became a basin, through which the present-day {{ill|lt=Oita River|Q11432686|WD=Q11432686|1=Ōita-gawa}} now flows. The strong man was named “Michinomi no Mikoto” and is still enshrined at the subsidiary Kerisaki Gongen Shrine. It is also said that the remnant of the dried lake became {{ill|lt=Lake Kinrin|Q11648024|WD=Q11648024|1=Lake Kinrin}}. However, archaeological and geological evidence has not confirmed the lake legend. Pottery has been excavated from the basin floor, wooden fragments dating back 23,000 years were discovered 20–30 meters underground, and no diatomaceous earth—expected if it had been a lake—has been found. As for the interpretation of the tradition, some identify Unaguhime with “Hayatsuhime,” mentioned in the {{ill|lt=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|Q11633698|WD=Q11633698|1=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki}}, while others point to the {{ill|lt=Himehiko system|Q11330202|WD=Q11330202|1=Hime-Hiko System}}. A similar tradition is also told of Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto in the Aso region. According to the ancient document {{ill|lt=Uetsufumi|Q11360118|WD=Q11360118|1=Uetsufumi}}, discovered in Oita Prefecture (Kamakura period, authored by {{ill|lt=Otomo Yoshinao|Q11433255|WD=Q11433255|1=Ōtomo Yoshinao}}, the first lord of Bungo Province, and generally considered a forgery), Unagihime was a deity governing cotton cultivation. The area was called Yufu (meaning cotton), suggesting that cotton was once cultivated there. However, cotton was introduced to Japan in the 8th century, and before that, “yū” (wooden cotton) usually referred to cloth made from tree fibers such as paper mulberry or hemp. --- === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in the 12th year of the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}. The “Shrine Register” records that Emperor Keikō conducted rituals here during his western expedition, and that in the third year of his reign, Hayatsuhime founded the shrine by imperial command. The shrine stands at the southwestern foothills of Mount Yufu. Historical sources indicate that it was originally established with Mount Yufu as its sacred object of worship (shintaisan). Another theory suggests that the shrine began with worship of eels as spirits based on the lake legend and later merged with the worship of Mount Yufu’s deity. === Overview === The shrine first appears in official history in 849 (Kashō 2), when it was granted the Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, and in 883 (Gangyō 7), it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. These ranks were conferred upon “Unaguhime no Kami.” In the mid-Heian period, the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} lists “Unaguhime Shrine” in Hayami District, Bungo Province, classifying it as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}. Until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was syncretized with {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} under the system of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinto-Buddhist syncretism|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, but following the {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, it assumed its present form. In 1873 (Meiji 6), it was designated a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in 1923 (Taishō 12), it was promoted to prefectural shrine status. === Divine Ranks === * 849 (Kashō 2), 6th month: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}) – listed as “Unaguhime no Kami.” * September 2, 883 (Gangyō 7): Promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}) – listed as “Unagihime no Kami.” --- === Shrine Precincts === The precincts exceed 10,000 tsubo and were once surrounded by ancient {{ill|lt=cedar trees|Q147388|WD=Q147388|1=Cryptomeria}}, but many were destroyed by a {{ill|lt=typhoon|Q140588|WD=Q140588|1=Typhoon}} in 1991 (Heisei 3). The main hall is a vermilion {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style structure. A large torii gate also stands in central Yufuin. File:Unaguhime-jinja honden.JPG|Main hall File:Unaguhime-jinja haiden-2.JPG|Worship hall File:Unaguhime-jinja shinmon.JPG|Approach and shrine gate File:Unaguhime-jinja torii.JPG|Torii gate --- === Subsidiary Shrines === '''Within the precincts''' * Tadasu Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Otoshi Shrine '''Outside the precincts''' * Ogo Shrine * Location: Kawaminami, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * The grounds contain many giant cedars, including the nationally designated natural monument “Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine,” over 1,000 years old and 38 meters tall (designated August 9, 1934). * Kerisaki Gongen Shrine * Location: Nakagawa, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * Enshrines the deity associated with the Kerisaki legend. --- === Cultural Properties === === National Natural Monument === * Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine – Located within the grounds of Ogo Shrine. Over 1,000 years old, 38 meters tall. Designated August 9, 1934. --- === Access Information === '''Address''' * 2220 Kawakami, Yufuin-chō, {{ill|lt=Yufu City|Q990455|WD=Q990455|1=Yufu}}, {{ill|lt=Oita Prefecture|Q133924|WD=Q133924|1=Ōita Prefecture}} '''Access''' * By train: {{ill|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|Q498366|WD=Q498366|lt=JR Kyushu|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Kyūdai Main Line|Q870569|WD=Q870569|1=Kyūdai Main Line}} – {{ill|lt=Yufuin Station|Q8060317|WD=Q8060317|1=Yufuin Station}} (approx. 15 minutes on foot) --- === References === '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="note"}} '''Sources''' {{reflist}} === Related Articles === {{Commonscat|Unaguhime-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}} * {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} – Former {{ill|lt=bettō temple|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} of this shrine. {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bungo Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:豊後国の式内社]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:由布市の建築物]] [[Category:由布市の歴史]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is also called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokushogu|1=UNKNOWN}},” and is commonly known as “Yufu Shrine” or “Yufusan Shrine.” === Enshrined Deities === The shrine enshrines the following six deities. The alternate name “Rokushogu” derives from worshipping these six deities: * {{ill|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|Q478504|WD=Q478504|lt=Kuni no Tokotachi no Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}} * {{ill|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|Q5966692|WD=Q5966692|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|1=Hoori}} * {{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Hikonagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|lt=Kamuyamato Iwarehiko no Mikoto|1=Emperor Jimmu}} (First Emperor Jimmu) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Suizei|Q313321|WD=Q313321|lt=Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto|1=Emperor Suizei}} (Second Emperor Suizei) --- === About Unaguhime === [[File:Yufuin Basin 11.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} (from the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}})
The basin was once a lake, said to have been developed by Unaguhime.]] Although the currently enshrined deities are the six listed above, the shrine name recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} is “Unaguhime Shrine,” and the conferment of divine ranks in the Six National Histories was made to “Unaguhime no Kami (Unagihime no Kami).” Therefore, it is believed that the original deity was '''Unaguhime''' (also read Unagihime or Unakihime). Regarding the name “Unaguhime,” one theory holds that “unagu” refers to a necklace such as magatama beads, suggesting that a female chieftain shaman wearing such ritual ornaments later became deified. Another theory derives the name from “unagi” (eel). There is an old legend concerning Unaguhime that the {{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} was once a lake (the “Kerisaki legend”). According to this story, Unaguhime, the deity of Mount Yufu, saw the lake spread before her and ordered a strong man to kick through its shore. When he did so, the water drained away and the lake became a basin, through which the present-day {{ill|lt=Oita River|Q11432686|WD=Q11432686|1=Ōita-gawa}} now flows. The strong man was named “Michinomi no Mikoto” and is still enshrined at the subsidiary Kerisaki Gongen Shrine. It is also said that the remnant of the dried lake became {{ill|lt=Lake Kinrin|Q11648024|WD=Q11648024|1=Lake Kinrin}}. However, archaeological and geological evidence has not confirmed the lake legend. Pottery has been excavated from the basin floor, wooden fragments dating back 23,000 years were discovered 20–30 meters underground, and no diatomaceous earth—expected if it had been a lake—has been found. As for the interpretation of the tradition, some identify Unaguhime with “Hayatsuhime,” mentioned in the {{ill|lt=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|Q11633698|WD=Q11633698|1=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki}}, while others point to the {{ill|lt=Himehiko system|Q11330202|WD=Q11330202|1=Hime-Hiko System}}. A similar tradition is also told of Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto in the Aso region. According to the ancient document {{ill|lt=Uetsufumi|Q11360118|WD=Q11360118|1=Uetsufumi}}, discovered in Oita Prefecture (Kamakura period, authored by {{ill|lt=Otomo Yoshinao|Q11433255|WD=Q11433255|1=Ōtomo Yoshinao}}, the first lord of Bungo Province, and generally considered a forgery), Unagihime was a deity governing cotton cultivation. The area was called Yufu (meaning cotton), suggesting that cotton was once cultivated there. However, cotton was introduced to Japan in the 8th century, and before that, “yū” (wooden cotton) usually referred to cloth made from tree fibers such as paper mulberry or hemp. --- === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in the 12th year of the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}. The “Shrine Register” records that Emperor Keikō conducted rituals here during his western expedition, and that in the third year of his reign, Hayatsuhime founded the shrine by imperial command. The shrine stands at the southwestern foothills of Mount Yufu. Historical sources indicate that it was originally established with Mount Yufu as its sacred object of worship (shintaisan). Another theory suggests that the shrine began with worship of eels as spirits based on the lake legend and later merged with the worship of Mount Yufu’s deity. === Overview === The shrine first appears in official history in 849 (Kashō 2), when it was granted the Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, and in 883 (Gangyō 7), it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. These ranks were conferred upon “Unaguhime no Kami.” In the mid-Heian period, the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} lists “Unaguhime Shrine” in Hayami District, Bungo Province, classifying it as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}. Until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was syncretized with {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} under the system of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinto-Buddhist syncretism|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, but following the {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, it assumed its present form. In 1873 (Meiji 6), it was designated a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in 1923 (Taishō 12), it was promoted to prefectural shrine status. === Divine Ranks === * 849 (Kashō 2), 6th month: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}) – listed as “Unaguhime no Kami.” * September 2, 883 (Gangyō 7): Promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}) – listed as “Unagihime no Kami.” --- === Shrine Precincts === The precincts exceed 10,000 tsubo and were once surrounded by ancient {{ill|lt=cedar trees|Q147388|WD=Q147388|1=Cryptomeria}}, but many were destroyed by a {{ill|lt=typhoon|Q140588|WD=Q140588|1=Typhoon}} in 1991 (Heisei 3). The main hall is a vermilion {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style structure. A large torii gate also stands in central Yufuin. File:Unaguhime-jinja honden.JPG|Main hall File:Unaguhime-jinja haiden-2.JPG|Worship hall File:Unaguhime-jinja shinmon.JPG|Approach and shrine gate File:Unaguhime-jinja torii.JPG|Torii gate --- === Subsidiary Shrines === '''Within the precincts''' * Tadasu Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Otoshi Shrine '''Outside the precincts''' * Ogo Shrine * Location: Kawaminami, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * The grounds contain many giant cedars, including the nationally designated natural monument “Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine,” over 1,000 years old and 38 meters tall (designated August 9, 1934). * Kerisaki Gongen Shrine * Location: Nakagawa, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * Enshrines the deity associated with the Kerisaki legend. --- === Cultural Properties === === National Natural Monument === * Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine – Located within the grounds of Ogo Shrine. Over 1,000 years old, 38 meters tall. Designated August 9, 1934. --- === Access Information === '''Address''' * 2220 Kawakami, Yufuin-chō, {{ill|lt=Yufu City|Q990455|WD=Q990455|1=Yufu}}, {{ill|lt=Oita Prefecture|Q133924|WD=Q133924|1=Ōita Prefecture}} '''Access''' * By train: {{ill|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|Q498366|WD=Q498366|lt=JR Kyushu|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Kyūdai Main Line|Q870569|WD=Q870569|1=Kyūdai Main Line}} – {{ill|lt=Yufuin Station|Q8060317|WD=Q8060317|1=Yufuin Station}} (approx. 15 minutes on foot) --- === References === '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="note"}} '''Sources''' {{reflist}} === Related Articles === {{Commonscat|Unaguhime-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}} * {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} – Former {{ill|lt=bettō temple|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} of this shrine. {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bungo Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bungo Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:由布市の建築物]] [[Category:由布市の歴史]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{List of coordinates}} [[File:Unaguhime-jinja ootorii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Great torii gate and {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}}]] '''Unaguhime Shrine''' (also read Unaguhime-jinja / Unakihime / Unagihime) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Yufu City|Q990455|WD=Q990455|1=Yufu}}, {{ill|lt=Oita Prefecture|Q133924|WD=Q133924|1=Ōita Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is also called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokushogu|1=UNKNOWN}},” and is commonly known as “Yufu Shrine” or “Yufusan Shrine.” === Enshrined Deities === The shrine enshrines the following six deities. The alternate name “Rokushogu” derives from worshipping these six deities: * {{ill|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|Q478504|WD=Q478504|lt=Kuni no Tokotachi no Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}} * {{ill|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|Q5966692|WD=Q5966692|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|1=Hoori}} * {{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Hikonagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|lt=Kamuyamato Iwarehiko no Mikoto|1=Emperor Jimmu}} (First Emperor Jimmu) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Suizei|Q313321|WD=Q313321|lt=Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto|1=Emperor Suizei}} (Second Emperor Suizei) --- === About Unaguhime === [[File:Yufuin Basin 11.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} (from the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}})
The basin was once a lake, said to have been developed by Unaguhime.]] Although the currently enshrined deities are the six listed above, the shrine name recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} is “Unaguhime Shrine,” and the conferment of divine ranks in the Six National Histories was made to “Unaguhime no Kami (Unagihime no Kami).” Therefore, it is believed that the original deity was '''Unaguhime''' (also read Unagihime or Unakihime). Regarding the name “Unaguhime,” one theory holds that “unagu” refers to a necklace such as magatama beads, suggesting that a female chieftain shaman wearing such ritual ornaments later became deified. Another theory derives the name from “unagi” (eel). There is an old legend concerning Unaguhime that the {{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} was once a lake (the “Kerisaki legend”). According to this story, Unaguhime, the deity of Mount Yufu, saw the lake spread before her and ordered a strong man to kick through its shore. When he did so, the water drained away and the lake became a basin, through which the present-day {{ill|lt=Oita River|Q11432686|WD=Q11432686|1=Ōita-gawa}} now flows. The strong man was named “Michinomi no Mikoto” and is still enshrined at the subsidiary Kerisaki Gongen Shrine. It is also said that the remnant of the dried lake became {{ill|lt=Lake Kinrin|Q11648024|WD=Q11648024|1=Lake Kinrin}}. However, archaeological and geological evidence has not confirmed the lake legend. Pottery has been excavated from the basin floor, wooden fragments dating back 23,000 years were discovered 20–30 meters underground, and no diatomaceous earth—expected if it had been a lake—has been found. As for the interpretation of the tradition, some identify Unaguhime with “Hayatsuhime,” mentioned in the {{ill|lt=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|Q11633698|WD=Q11633698|1=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki}}, while others point to the {{ill|lt=Himehiko system|Q11330202|WD=Q11330202|1=Hime-Hiko System}}. A similar tradition is also told of Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto in the Aso region. According to the ancient document {{ill|lt=Uetsufumi|Q11360118|WD=Q11360118|1=Uetsufumi}}, discovered in Oita Prefecture (Kamakura period, authored by {{ill|lt=Otomo Yoshinao|Q11433255|WD=Q11433255|1=Ōtomo Yoshinao}}, the first lord of Bungo Province, and generally considered a forgery), Unagihime was a deity governing cotton cultivation. The area was called Yufu (meaning cotton), suggesting that cotton was once cultivated there. However, cotton was introduced to Japan in the 8th century, and before that, “yū” (wooden cotton) usually referred to cloth made from tree fibers such as paper mulberry or hemp. --- === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in the 12th year of the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}. The “Shrine Register” records that Emperor Keikō conducted rituals here during his western expedition, and that in the third year of his reign, Hayatsuhime founded the shrine by imperial command. The shrine stands at the southwestern foothills of Mount Yufu. Historical sources indicate that it was originally established with Mount Yufu as its sacred object of worship (shintaisan). Another theory suggests that the shrine began with worship of eels as spirits based on the lake legend and later merged with the worship of Mount Yufu’s deity. === Overview === The shrine first appears in official history in 849 (Kashō 2), when it was granted the Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, and in 883 (Gangyō 7), it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. These ranks were conferred upon “Unaguhime no Kami.” In the mid-Heian period, the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} lists “Unaguhime Shrine” in Hayami District, Bungo Province, classifying it as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}. Until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was syncretized with {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} under the system of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinto-Buddhist syncretism|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, but following the {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, it assumed its present form. In 1873 (Meiji 6), it was designated a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in 1923 (Taishō 12), it was promoted to prefectural shrine status. === Divine Ranks === * 849 (Kashō 2), 6th month: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}) – listed as “Unaguhime no Kami.” * September 2, 883 (Gangyō 7): Promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}) – listed as “Unagihime no Kami.” --- === Shrine Precincts === The precincts exceed 10,000 tsubo and were once surrounded by ancient {{ill|lt=cedar trees|Q147388|WD=Q147388|1=Cryptomeria}}, but many were destroyed by a {{ill|lt=typhoon|Q140588|WD=Q140588|1=Typhoon}} in 1991 (Heisei 3). The main hall is a vermilion {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style structure. A large torii gate also stands in central Yufuin. File:Unaguhime-jinja honden.JPG|Main hall File:Unaguhime-jinja haiden-2.JPG|Worship hall File:Unaguhime-jinja shinmon.JPG|Approach and shrine gate File:Unaguhime-jinja torii.JPG|Torii gate --- === Subsidiary Shrines === '''Within the precincts''' * Tadasu Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Otoshi Shrine '''Outside the precincts''' * Ogo Shrine * Location: Kawaminami, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * The grounds contain many giant cedars, including the nationally designated natural monument “Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine,” over 1,000 years old and 38 meters tall (designated August 9, 1934). * Kerisaki Gongen Shrine * Location: Nakagawa, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * Enshrines the deity associated with the Kerisaki legend. --- === Cultural Properties === === National Natural Monument === * Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine – Located within the grounds of Ogo Shrine. Over 1,000 years old, 38 meters tall. Designated August 9, 1934. --- === Access Information === '''Address''' * 2220 Kawakami, Yufuin-chō, {{ill|lt=Yufu City|Q990455|WD=Q990455|1=Yufu}}, {{ill|lt=Oita Prefecture|Q133924|WD=Q133924|1=Ōita Prefecture}} '''Access''' * By train: {{ill|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|Q498366|WD=Q498366|lt=JR Kyushu|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Kyūdai Main Line|Q870569|WD=Q870569|1=Kyūdai Main Line}} – {{ill|lt=Yufuin Station|Q8060317|WD=Q8060317|1=Yufuin Station}} (approx. 15 minutes on foot) --- === References === '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="note"}} '''Sources''' {{reflist}} === Related Articles === {{Commonscat|Unaguhime-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}} * {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} – Former {{ill|lt=bettō temple|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} of this shrine. {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bungo Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bungo Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:由布市の建築物]] [[Category:由布市の歴史]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded = (Traditionally) 12th year of the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Other names = Rokushogu, Yufu Shrine |Festival = {{ill|lt=September 22|Q2889|WD=Q2889|1=September 22}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Oita|label=Unaguhime
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{List of coordinates}} [[File:Unaguhime-jinja ootorii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Great torii gate and {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}}]] '''Unaguhime Shrine''' (also read Unaguhime-jinja / Unakihime / Unagihime) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Yufu City|Q990455|WD=Q990455|1=Yufu}}, {{ill|lt=Oita Prefecture|Q133924|WD=Q133924|1=Ōita Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is also called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokushogu|1=UNKNOWN}},” and is commonly known as “Yufu Shrine” or “Yufusan Shrine.” === Enshrined Deities === The shrine enshrines the following six deities. The alternate name “Rokushogu” derives from worshipping these six deities: * {{ill|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|Q478504|WD=Q478504|lt=Kuni no Tokotachi no Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}} * {{ill|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|Q5966692|WD=Q5966692|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|1=Hoori}} * {{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Hikonagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|lt=Kamuyamato Iwarehiko no Mikoto|1=Emperor Jimmu}} (First Emperor Jimmu) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Suizei|Q313321|WD=Q313321|lt=Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto|1=Emperor Suizei}} (Second Emperor Suizei) --- === About Unaguhime === [[File:Yufuin Basin 11.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} (from the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}})
The basin was once a lake, said to have been developed by Unaguhime.]] Although the currently enshrined deities are the six listed above, the shrine name recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} is “Unaguhime Shrine,” and the conferment of divine ranks in the Six National Histories was made to “Unaguhime no Kami (Unagihime no Kami).” Therefore, it is believed that the original deity was '''Unaguhime''' (also read Unagihime or Unakihime). Regarding the name “Unaguhime,” one theory holds that “unagu” refers to a necklace such as magatama beads, suggesting that a female chieftain shaman wearing such ritual ornaments later became deified. Another theory derives the name from “unagi” (eel). There is an old legend concerning Unaguhime that the {{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} was once a lake (the “Kerisaki legend”). According to this story, Unaguhime, the deity of Mount Yufu, saw the lake spread before her and ordered a strong man to kick through its shore. When he did so, the water drained away and the lake became a basin, through which the present-day {{ill|lt=Oita River|Q11432686|WD=Q11432686|1=Ōita-gawa}} now flows. The strong man was named “Michinomi no Mikoto” and is still enshrined at the subsidiary Kerisaki Gongen Shrine. It is also said that the remnant of the dried lake became {{ill|lt=Lake Kinrin|Q11648024|WD=Q11648024|1=Lake Kinrin}}. However, archaeological and geological evidence has not confirmed the lake legend. Pottery has been excavated from the basin floor, wooden fragments dating back 23,000 years were discovered 20–30 meters underground, and no diatomaceous earth—expected if it had been a lake—has been found. As for the interpretation of the tradition, some identify Unaguhime with “Hayatsuhime,” mentioned in the {{ill|lt=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|Q11633698|WD=Q11633698|1=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki}}, while others point to the {{ill|lt=Himehiko system|Q11330202|WD=Q11330202|1=Hime-Hiko System}}. A similar tradition is also told of Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto in the Aso region. According to the ancient document {{ill|lt=Uetsufumi|Q11360118|WD=Q11360118|1=Uetsufumi}}, discovered in Oita Prefecture (Kamakura period, authored by {{ill|lt=Otomo Yoshinao|Q11433255|WD=Q11433255|1=Ōtomo Yoshinao}}, the first lord of Bungo Province, and generally considered a forgery), Unagihime was a deity governing cotton cultivation. The area was called Yufu (meaning cotton), suggesting that cotton was once cultivated there. However, cotton was introduced to Japan in the 8th century, and before that, “yū” (wooden cotton) usually referred to cloth made from tree fibers such as paper mulberry or hemp. --- === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in the 12th year of the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}. The “Shrine Register” records that Emperor Keikō conducted rituals here during his western expedition, and that in the third year of his reign, Hayatsuhime founded the shrine by imperial command. The shrine stands at the southwestern foothills of Mount Yufu. Historical sources indicate that it was originally established with Mount Yufu as its sacred object of worship (shintaisan). Another theory suggests that the shrine began with worship of eels as spirits based on the lake legend and later merged with the worship of Mount Yufu’s deity. === Overview === The shrine first appears in official history in 849 (Kashō 2), when it was granted the Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, and in 883 (Gangyō 7), it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. These ranks were conferred upon “Unaguhime no Kami.” In the mid-Heian period, the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} lists “Unaguhime Shrine” in Hayami District, Bungo Province, classifying it as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}. Until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was syncretized with {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} under the system of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinto-Buddhist syncretism|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, but following the {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, it assumed its present form. In 1873 (Meiji 6), it was designated a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in 1923 (Taishō 12), it was promoted to prefectural shrine status. === Divine Ranks === * 849 (Kashō 2), 6th month: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}) – listed as “Unaguhime no Kami.” * September 2, 883 (Gangyō 7): Promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}) – listed as “Unagihime no Kami.” --- === Shrine Precincts === The precincts exceed 10,000 tsubo and were once surrounded by ancient {{ill|lt=cedar trees|Q147388|WD=Q147388|1=Cryptomeria}}, but many were destroyed by a {{ill|lt=typhoon|Q140588|WD=Q140588|1=Typhoon}} in 1991 (Heisei 3). The main hall is a vermilion {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style structure. A large torii gate also stands in central Yufuin. File:Unaguhime-jinja honden.JPG|Main hall File:Unaguhime-jinja haiden-2.JPG|Worship hall File:Unaguhime-jinja shinmon.JPG|Approach and shrine gate File:Unaguhime-jinja torii.JPG|Torii gate --- === Subsidiary Shrines === '''Within the precincts''' * Tadasu Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Otoshi Shrine '''Outside the precincts''' * Ogo Shrine * Location: Kawaminami, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * The grounds contain many giant cedars, including the nationally designated natural monument “Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine,” over 1,000 years old and 38 meters tall (designated August 9, 1934). * Kerisaki Gongen Shrine * Location: Nakagawa, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * Enshrines the deity associated with the Kerisaki legend. --- === Cultural Properties === === National Natural Monument === * Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine – Located within the grounds of Ogo Shrine. Over 1,000 years old, 38 meters tall. Designated August 9, 1934. --- === Access Information === '''Address''' * 2220 Kawakami, Yufuin-chō, {{ill|lt=Yufu City|Q990455|WD=Q990455|1=Yufu}}, {{ill|lt=Oita Prefecture|Q133924|WD=Q133924|1=Ōita Prefecture}} '''Access''' * By train: {{ill|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|Q498366|WD=Q498366|lt=JR Kyushu|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Kyūdai Main Line|Q870569|WD=Q870569|1=Kyūdai Main Line}} – {{ill|lt=Yufuin Station|Q8060317|WD=Q8060317|1=Yufuin Station}} (approx. 15 minutes on foot) --- === References === '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="note"}} '''Sources''' {{reflist}} === Related Articles === {{Commonscat|Unaguhime-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}} * {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} – Former {{ill|lt=bettō temple|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} of this shrine. {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bungo Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bungo Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:由布市の建築物]] [[Category:由布市の歴史]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

{{ill|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|Q5966692|WD=Q5966692|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto}}

{{ill|lt=Emperor Suizei|Q313321|WD=Q313321|lt=Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto|1=Emperor Suizei}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded = (Traditionally) 12th year of the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Other names = Rokushogu, Yufu Shrine |Festival = {{ill|lt=September 22|Q2889|WD=Q2889|1=September 22}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Oita|label=Unaguhime
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{List of coordinates}} [[File:Unaguhime-jinja ootorii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Great torii gate and {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}}]] '''Unaguhime Shrine''' (also read Unaguhime-jinja / Unakihime / Unagihime) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Yufu City|Q990455|WD=Q990455|1=Yufu}}, {{ill|lt=Oita Prefecture|Q133924|WD=Q133924|1=Ōita Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is also called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokushogu|1=UNKNOWN}},” and is commonly known as “Yufu Shrine” or “Yufusan Shrine.” === Enshrined Deities === The shrine enshrines the following six deities. The alternate name “Rokushogu” derives from worshipping these six deities: * {{ill|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|Q478504|WD=Q478504|lt=Kuni no Tokotachi no Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}} * {{ill|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|Q5966692|WD=Q5966692|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|1=Hoori}} * {{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Hikonagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|lt=Kamuyamato Iwarehiko no Mikoto|1=Emperor Jimmu}} (First Emperor Jimmu) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Suizei|Q313321|WD=Q313321|lt=Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto|1=Emperor Suizei}} (Second Emperor Suizei) --- === About Unaguhime === [[File:Yufuin Basin 11.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} (from the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}})
The basin was once a lake, said to have been developed by Unaguhime.]] Although the currently enshrined deities are the six listed above, the shrine name recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} is “Unaguhime Shrine,” and the conferment of divine ranks in the Six National Histories was made to “Unaguhime no Kami (Unagihime no Kami).” Therefore, it is believed that the original deity was '''Unaguhime''' (also read Unagihime or Unakihime). Regarding the name “Unaguhime,” one theory holds that “unagu” refers to a necklace such as magatama beads, suggesting that a female chieftain shaman wearing such ritual ornaments later became deified. Another theory derives the name from “unagi” (eel). There is an old legend concerning Unaguhime that the {{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} was once a lake (the “Kerisaki legend”). According to this story, Unaguhime, the deity of Mount Yufu, saw the lake spread before her and ordered a strong man to kick through its shore. When he did so, the water drained away and the lake became a basin, through which the present-day {{ill|lt=Oita River|Q11432686|WD=Q11432686|1=Ōita-gawa}} now flows. The strong man was named “Michinomi no Mikoto” and is still enshrined at the subsidiary Kerisaki Gongen Shrine. It is also said that the remnant of the dried lake became {{ill|lt=Lake Kinrin|Q11648024|WD=Q11648024|1=Lake Kinrin}}. However, archaeological and geological evidence has not confirmed the lake legend. Pottery has been excavated from the basin floor, wooden fragments dating back 23,000 years were discovered 20–30 meters underground, and no diatomaceous earth—expected if it had been a lake—has been found. As for the interpretation of the tradition, some identify Unaguhime with “Hayatsuhime,” mentioned in the {{ill|lt=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|Q11633698|WD=Q11633698|1=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki}}, while others point to the {{ill|lt=Himehiko system|Q11330202|WD=Q11330202|1=Hime-Hiko System}}. A similar tradition is also told of Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto in the Aso region. According to the ancient document {{ill|lt=Uetsufumi|Q11360118|WD=Q11360118|1=Uetsufumi}}, discovered in Oita Prefecture (Kamakura period, authored by {{ill|lt=Otomo Yoshinao|Q11433255|WD=Q11433255|1=Ōtomo Yoshinao}}, the first lord of Bungo Province, and generally considered a forgery), Unagihime was a deity governing cotton cultivation. The area was called Yufu (meaning cotton), suggesting that cotton was once cultivated there. However, cotton was introduced to Japan in the 8th century, and before that, “yū” (wooden cotton) usually referred to cloth made from tree fibers such as paper mulberry or hemp. --- === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in the 12th year of the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}. The “Shrine Register” records that Emperor Keikō conducted rituals here during his western expedition, and that in the third year of his reign, Hayatsuhime founded the shrine by imperial command. The shrine stands at the southwestern foothills of Mount Yufu. Historical sources indicate that it was originally established with Mount Yufu as its sacred object of worship (shintaisan). Another theory suggests that the shrine began with worship of eels as spirits based on the lake legend and later merged with the worship of Mount Yufu’s deity. === Overview === The shrine first appears in official history in 849 (Kashō 2), when it was granted the Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, and in 883 (Gangyō 7), it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. These ranks were conferred upon “Unaguhime no Kami.” In the mid-Heian period, the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} lists “Unaguhime Shrine” in Hayami District, Bungo Province, classifying it as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}. Until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was syncretized with {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} under the system of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinto-Buddhist syncretism|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, but following the {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, it assumed its present form. In 1873 (Meiji 6), it was designated a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in 1923 (Taishō 12), it was promoted to prefectural shrine status. === Divine Ranks === * 849 (Kashō 2), 6th month: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}) – listed as “Unaguhime no Kami.” * September 2, 883 (Gangyō 7): Promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}) – listed as “Unagihime no Kami.” --- === Shrine Precincts === The precincts exceed 10,000 tsubo and were once surrounded by ancient {{ill|lt=cedar trees|Q147388|WD=Q147388|1=Cryptomeria}}, but many were destroyed by a {{ill|lt=typhoon|Q140588|WD=Q140588|1=Typhoon}} in 1991 (Heisei 3). The main hall is a vermilion {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style structure. A large torii gate also stands in central Yufuin. File:Unaguhime-jinja honden.JPG|Main hall File:Unaguhime-jinja haiden-2.JPG|Worship hall File:Unaguhime-jinja shinmon.JPG|Approach and shrine gate File:Unaguhime-jinja torii.JPG|Torii gate --- === Subsidiary Shrines === '''Within the precincts''' * Tadasu Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Otoshi Shrine '''Outside the precincts''' * Ogo Shrine * Location: Kawaminami, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * The grounds contain many giant cedars, including the nationally designated natural monument “Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine,” over 1,000 years old and 38 meters tall (designated August 9, 1934). * Kerisaki Gongen Shrine * Location: Nakagawa, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * Enshrines the deity associated with the Kerisaki legend. --- === Cultural Properties === === National Natural Monument === * Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine – Located within the grounds of Ogo Shrine. Over 1,000 years old, 38 meters tall. Designated August 9, 1934. --- === Access Information === '''Address''' * 2220 Kawakami, Yufuin-chō, {{ill|lt=Yufu City|Q990455|WD=Q990455|1=Yufu}}, {{ill|lt=Oita Prefecture|Q133924|WD=Q133924|1=Ōita Prefecture}} '''Access''' * By train: {{ill|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|Q498366|WD=Q498366|lt=JR Kyushu|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Kyūdai Main Line|Q870569|WD=Q870569|1=Kyūdai Main Line}} – {{ill|lt=Yufuin Station|Q8060317|WD=Q8060317|1=Yufuin Station}} (approx. 15 minutes on foot) --- === References === '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="note"}} '''Sources''' {{reflist}} === Related Articles === {{Commonscat|Unaguhime-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}} * {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} – Former {{ill|lt=bettō temple|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} of this shrine. {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bungo Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bungo Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:由布市の建築物]] [[Category:由布市の歴史]]

{{ill|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|Q5966692|WD=Q5966692|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|1=Hoori}}
{{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Hikonagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}}
{{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|lt=Kamuyamato Iwarehiko no Mikoto|1=Emperor Jimmu}}
{{ill|lt=Emperor Suizei|Q313321|WD=Q313321|lt=Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto|1=Emperor Suizei}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded = (Traditionally) 12th year of the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} |Main hall style = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Other names = Rokushogu, Yufu Shrine |Festival = {{ill|lt=September 22|Q2889|WD=Q2889|1=September 22}} |Map = {{Location map|Japan Oita|label=Unaguhime
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{List of coordinates}} [[File:Unaguhime-jinja ootorii.JPG|thumb|250px|right|Great torii gate and {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}}]] '''Unaguhime Shrine''' (also read Unaguhime-jinja / Unakihime / Unagihime) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Yufu City|Q990455|WD=Q990455|1=Yufu}}, {{ill|lt=Oita Prefecture|Q133924|WD=Q133924|1=Ōita Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is also called “{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Rokushogu|1=UNKNOWN}},” and is commonly known as “Yufu Shrine” or “Yufusan Shrine.” === Enshrined Deities === The shrine enshrines the following six deities. The alternate name “Rokushogu” derives from worshipping these six deities: * {{ill|WD=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Mikoto|Q478504|WD=Q478504|lt=Kuni no Tokotachi no Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}} * {{ill|lt=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto|Q5966692|WD=Q5966692|1=Kunisatsuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Hoori|Q1051960|WD=Q1051960|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|1=Hoori}} * {{ill|lt=Ugaya Fukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Hikonagisatake Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188|lt=Kamuyamato Iwarehiko no Mikoto|1=Emperor Jimmu}} (First Emperor Jimmu) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Suizei|Q313321|WD=Q313321|lt=Kamununakawamimi no Mikoto|1=Emperor Suizei}} (Second Emperor Suizei) --- === About Unaguhime === [[File:Yufuin Basin 11.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} (from the summit of {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}})
The basin was once a lake, said to have been developed by Unaguhime.]] Although the currently enshrined deities are the six listed above, the shrine name recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} is “Unaguhime Shrine,” and the conferment of divine ranks in the Six National Histories was made to “Unaguhime no Kami (Unagihime no Kami).” Therefore, it is believed that the original deity was '''Unaguhime''' (also read Unagihime or Unakihime). Regarding the name “Unaguhime,” one theory holds that “unagu” refers to a necklace such as magatama beads, suggesting that a female chieftain shaman wearing such ritual ornaments later became deified. Another theory derives the name from “unagi” (eel). There is an old legend concerning Unaguhime that the {{ill|lt=Yufuin Basin|Q11577584|WD=Q11577584|1=Yufuin Basin}} was once a lake (the “Kerisaki legend”). According to this story, Unaguhime, the deity of Mount Yufu, saw the lake spread before her and ordered a strong man to kick through its shore. When he did so, the water drained away and the lake became a basin, through which the present-day {{ill|lt=Oita River|Q11432686|WD=Q11432686|1=Ōita-gawa}} now flows. The strong man was named “Michinomi no Mikoto” and is still enshrined at the subsidiary Kerisaki Gongen Shrine. It is also said that the remnant of the dried lake became {{ill|lt=Lake Kinrin|Q11648024|WD=Q11648024|1=Lake Kinrin}}. However, archaeological and geological evidence has not confirmed the lake legend. Pottery has been excavated from the basin floor, wooden fragments dating back 23,000 years were discovered 20–30 meters underground, and no diatomaceous earth—expected if it had been a lake—has been found. As for the interpretation of the tradition, some identify Unaguhime with “Hayatsuhime,” mentioned in the {{ill|lt=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki|Q11633698|WD=Q11633698|1=Bungo no Kuni Fudoki}}, while others point to the {{ill|lt=Himehiko system|Q11330202|WD=Q11330202|1=Hime-Hiko System}}. A similar tradition is also told of Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto in the Aso region. According to the ancient document {{ill|lt=Uetsufumi|Q11360118|WD=Q11360118|1=Uetsufumi}}, discovered in Oita Prefecture (Kamakura period, authored by {{ill|lt=Otomo Yoshinao|Q11433255|WD=Q11433255|1=Ōtomo Yoshinao}}, the first lord of Bungo Province, and generally considered a forgery), Unagihime was a deity governing cotton cultivation. The area was called Yufu (meaning cotton), suggesting that cotton was once cultivated there. However, cotton was introduced to Japan in the 8th century, and before that, “yū” (wooden cotton) usually referred to cloth made from tree fibers such as paper mulberry or hemp. --- === History === === Founding === According to shrine tradition, the shrine was founded in the 12th year of the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keikō|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}. The “Shrine Register” records that Emperor Keikō conducted rituals here during his western expedition, and that in the third year of his reign, Hayatsuhime founded the shrine by imperial command. The shrine stands at the southwestern foothills of Mount Yufu. Historical sources indicate that it was originally established with Mount Yufu as its sacred object of worship (shintaisan). Another theory suggests that the shrine began with worship of eels as spirits based on the lake legend and later merged with the worship of Mount Yufu’s deity. === Overview === The shrine first appears in official history in 849 (Kashō 2), when it was granted the Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade, and in 883 (Gangyō 7), it was promoted to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. These ranks were conferred upon “Unaguhime no Kami.” In the mid-Heian period, the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Register of Shrines|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} lists “Unaguhime Shrine” in Hayami District, Bungo Province, classifying it as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}. Until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was syncretized with {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} under the system of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinto-Buddhist syncretism|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, but following the {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, it assumed its present form. In 1873 (Meiji 6), it was designated a village shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and in 1923 (Taishō 12), it was promoted to prefectural shrine status. === Divine Ranks === * 849 (Kashō 2), 6th month: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}) – listed as “Unaguhime no Kami.” * September 2, 883 (Gangyō 7): Promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade to Senior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (recorded in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}) – listed as “Unagihime no Kami.” --- === Shrine Precincts === The precincts exceed 10,000 tsubo and were once surrounded by ancient {{ill|lt=cedar trees|Q147388|WD=Q147388|1=Cryptomeria}}, but many were destroyed by a {{ill|lt=typhoon|Q140588|WD=Q140588|1=Typhoon}} in 1991 (Heisei 3). The main hall is a vermilion {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style structure. A large torii gate also stands in central Yufuin. File:Unaguhime-jinja honden.JPG|Main hall File:Unaguhime-jinja haiden-2.JPG|Worship hall File:Unaguhime-jinja shinmon.JPG|Approach and shrine gate File:Unaguhime-jinja torii.JPG|Torii gate --- === Subsidiary Shrines === '''Within the precincts''' * Tadasu Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Otoshi Shrine '''Outside the precincts''' * Ogo Shrine * Location: Kawaminami, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * The grounds contain many giant cedars, including the nationally designated natural monument “Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine,” over 1,000 years old and 38 meters tall (designated August 9, 1934). * Kerisaki Gongen Shrine * Location: Nakagawa, Yufuin-chō, Yufu City * Enshrines the deity associated with the Kerisaki legend. --- === Cultural Properties === === National Natural Monument === * Great Cedar of Ogo Shrine – Located within the grounds of Ogo Shrine. Over 1,000 years old, 38 meters tall. Designated August 9, 1934. --- === Access Information === '''Address''' * 2220 Kawakami, Yufuin-chō, {{ill|lt=Yufu City|Q990455|WD=Q990455|1=Yufu}}, {{ill|lt=Oita Prefecture|Q133924|WD=Q133924|1=Ōita Prefecture}} '''Access''' * By train: {{ill|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|Q498366|WD=Q498366|lt=JR Kyushu|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Kyūdai Main Line|Q870569|WD=Q870569|1=Kyūdai Main Line}} – {{ill|lt=Yufuin Station|Q8060317|WD=Q8060317|1=Yufuin Station}} (approx. 15 minutes on foot) --- === References === '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="note"}} '''Sources''' {{reflist}} === Related Articles === {{Commonscat|Unaguhime-jinja}} * {{ill|lt=Mount Yufu|Q705288|WD=Q705288|1=Mount Yufu}} * {{ill|lt=Busshōji Temple|Q11385308|WD=Q11385308|1=Bussan-ji Temple}} – Former {{ill|lt=bettō temple|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} of this shrine. {{Shinto}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Unaguhime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bungo Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Bungo Province]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:由布市の建築物]] [[Category:由布市の歴史]]
